topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The letter felt like a physical insult in my trembling hand. It arrived on a crisp Tuesday morning, the official notification that my years of grueling training, the early mornings, the sacrifices I’d made—it all amounted to nothing. I reread the rejection email three times, the words blurring into meaningless static. I slammed my laptop shut so hard the screen rattled. My jaw clenched so tight I could taste copper. I looked around my cramped apartment, at the framed photos of past victories, and felt a surge of pure, cold resentment bubble up. They had all been wrong. They simply didn't see the fire burning inside me. I started pacing, the floorboards groaning beneath my frantic steps, muttering curses under my breath, the sound raw and ugly in the quiet room.

[story 2]

"Just another disappointment," I whispered to the empty gym. The air in the cavernous space felt thick and suffocating. I watched the other prospects—the ones who got the call, the ones who were lauded—laughing and high-fiving. Their easy confidence grated on my nerves like sandpaper. I felt a burning sensation behind my eyes, a pressure building behind my ribs that made breathing shallow. When the coach finally walked past, his expression neutral, I didn't bother to look up. I just stared at the scuff marks on the floor, letting the simmering fury boil over into a silent, rigid posture. Every muscle in my body was coiled, ready to snap, fueled by a deep, bitter sense of being overlooked.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the final practice session in my mind, dissecting every missed move, every fraction of a second where I faltered. It wasn't just a loss; it felt like a deliberate slight. When the coach finally called my name, the sound didn't bring relief; it brought a sharp, stinging sense of betrayal. I stood there, my shoulders slumped, deliberately letting my disappointment manifest as a heavy, dead weight. I didn't offer an explanation, just a slow, deliberate turn away, my gaze fixed on the distant exit. The silence that followed was deafening, filled only with the pounding rhythm of my own frustrated heart, a relentless drumbeat of pure, unadulterated dissatisfaction.
